name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ لَّا يُحِبُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْجَهْرَ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَن ظُلِمَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ سَمِيعًا عَلِيمًا (١٤٨)
```

```english
Allah does not like the disclosure of [anyone’s] evil [conduct] in speech except by someone who has been wronged, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (148)
```

```arabic
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ خَيْرًا أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا۟ عَن سُوٓءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيرًا (١٤٩)
```

```english
Whether you disclose a good [deed that you do] or hide it, or excuse an evil [deed], Allah is indeed all-excusing, all-powerful. (149)
```

## (Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: **لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ بِالسُّوءِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَنْ ظُلِمَ** "Allah does not like the utterance of evil in public unless one has been wronged" (4:148). Al-Raghib said regarding the root **"جهر"** (public/manifest): "It is said of the manifestation of a thing to an excessive degree for the sense of sight or the sense of hearing. As for sight, it is like 'I saw him openly' (جهاراً); Allah the Exalted says: **لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى نَرَى اللهَ جَهْرَةً** 'We will not believe you until we see Allah visibly' (2:55), and **أَرِنَا اللهَ جَهْرَةً** 'Show us Allah visibly' (4:153)—until he said—and as for hearing, from it is His saying, the Exalted: **سَواءٌ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَسَرَّ الْقَوْلَ وَمَنْ جَهَرَ بِهِ** 'It is the same [to Him] whether any of you speaks secretly or aloud' (13:10)." Evil speech (السوء من القول) refers to every word that distresses the one about whom it is said, such as invoking evil against him, or reviling him for the faults and defects he possesses or those he does not. Allah does not like the public utterance (الجهر) or disclosure of any of this. It is well known that He, the Exalted, is transcendent above love (الحب) and hatred (البغض) in the sense found within us, the human collective, and the animals that resemble us. However, since command and prohibition in our case naturally stem from love and hatred, these terms are used as metaphors for His will (الإرادة) and dislike (الكراهة), and for His command and prohibition.

Thus, His saying: **لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ بِالسُّوءِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ** "Allah does not like the utterance of evil in public" is a metaphor for legislative dislike (الكراهة التشريعية), which is broader than both prohibition (التحريم) and disapproval (الكراهة).

His saying: **إِلَّا مَنْ ظُلِمَ** "unless one has been wronged" is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع); meaning: but he who has been wronged, there is no harm in him speaking evil publicly regarding the one who wronged him, in the context of the wrong committed. This serves as the contextual clue (القرينة) that he is only permitted to speak evil publicly to clarify the wrong done to him and to expose the faults of the wrongdoer that pertain to the injustice committed. As for transgressing beyond that to things the wrongdoer does not possess, or things unrelated to the injustice, there is no evidence in the verse for the permissibility of speaking such things publicly.

Although the exegetes have differed in interpreting "evil speech" (السوء من القول)—with some saying it is invoking evil against the wrongdoer, and others saying it is mentioning his injustice and his transgression against him, and so on—all of these are included in the general application (إطلاق) of the verse. Therefore, there is no reason to restrict the discourse to only some of them.

His saying: **وَكانَ اللهُ سَمِيعاً عَلِيماً** "and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" (4:148) is in the position of emphasizing the prohibition derived from His saying: **لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ** "Allah does not like the utterance... in public." That is, it is not fitting for anyone other than the wronged party to speak evil publicly, for Allah is All-hearing (سميع), hearing what is said, and All-knowing (عليم), knowing the intent behind it.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ تُبْدُوا خَيْراً أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا عَنْ سُوءٍ فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيراً** "Whether you reveal a good [deed] or hide it, or pardon an evil, Allah is indeed All-excusing, All-powerful" (4:149). This verse is not without a connection to the preceding one, for it encompasses the manifestation of good speech in gratitude for a blessing bestowed by a benefactor upon a person, and it encompasses pardoning evil and injustice so that one does not speak evil publicly against the wrongdoer.

**Revealing** (إبداء) good means making it manifest, whether it is an action—such as publicly spending on those who deserve it, and likewise every virtuous deed (معروف) because it involves exalting the word of the religion and encouraging people toward virtue—or whether it is a word, such as publicly thanking a benefactor and mentioning him with beautiful speech because it involves proper appreciation and encourages the people of bounty.

The **hiding** (إخفاء) of good refers to concealing the performance of a virtuous deed so that it may be further removed from ostentation (الرئاء) and closer to sincerity (الخلوص), as He said: **إِنْ تُبْدُوا الصَّدَقاتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِيَ وَإِنْ تُخْفُوها وَتُؤْتُوهَا الْفُقَراءَ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ وَيُكَفِّرُ عَنْكُمْ مِنْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** "If you disclose your charities, that is well, but if you hide them and give them to the poor, that is better for you, and it will atone for some of your misdeeds" (2:271).

**Pardoning** (العفو) an evil means covering it up verbally by not mentioning the wrongdoer's injustice, not tarnishing his reputation among the people, and not speaking evil against him publicly; and practically, by not confronting him with a response equivalent to his ill-treatment, and not taking revenge against him in matters where it would otherwise be permissible, as the Exalted said: **فَمَنِ اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ** "So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah" (2:194).

His saying: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيراً** "Allah is indeed All-excusing, All-powerful" is a cause placed in the position of the effect. The implied meaning is: if you pardon an evil, you have characterized yourselves with one of the perfect attributes (صفات كمالية) of Allah—which is pardoning (العفو) despite having power (القدرة)—for Allah is the Possessor of pardon despite His power. Thus, the recompense is a reward in relation to some of the conditions. As for revealing good and hiding it—meaning performing it in any state—this is also among His attributes, the Exalted, insofar as He is Allah, the Exalted, and the discourse may allude to this.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, it is said: "Allah does not like reviling in seeking redress except for one who has been wronged—so there is no harm for him to seek redress from the one who wronged him in ways permissible in the religion." He said: "And this is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu al-Jarud, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "The public utterance of evil speech is to mention a man for what is [actually] in him."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: "And in another tradition regarding the interpretation of this, he said: 'If a man comes to you and says regarding you what is not in you of good, praise, and righteous deeds—do not accept it from him and belie him, for he has wronged you.'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, with its chain of narration from al-Fadl ibn Abi Qurrah: from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: **لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ بِالسُّوءِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَنْ ظُلِمَ** "Allah does not like the utterance of evil in public unless one has been wronged," he said: "Whoever hosts a people and treats them poorly in his hospitality—he is among those who have committed a wrong, so there is no sin upon them in what they say about him." I say: It was also narrated in *al-Majma'* from him (peace be upon him) as a disconnected report (مرسلاً), and it is narrated through Sunni channels from Mujahid.

The narrations, in any case, indicate a general application (التعميم) as we derived from the verse.
